\chapter{Boolean Analysis — Switching}
%%% generated by the blueprint pipeline from TCSlib.BooleanAnalysis.Switching
\section{Overview}

This module assembles Håstad's Switching Lemma. Building on the Razborov-style
encoder and the canonical decision tree, it develops the combinatorial machinery
needed to count the restrictions $\rho$ under which a width-$w$ DNF $f$ restricts
to a function of large decision-tree depth, proves the path-consumption and
free-variable invariants of the encoder, and concludes both the DNF and (by De
Morgan duality) the CNF versions of the switching lemma together with their
representation-size corollaries.

\section{Declarations}

\begin{definition}[Parse an aux list into triples]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux}
\leanok
Parses a low-level aux list of $(\mathbb{N} \times \mathrm{Bool})$ entries into
a list of triples $(\mathrm{pos}, \mathrm{dir}, \mathrm{hasMarker})$ with
$\mathrm{pos} : \mathrm{Fin}\,w$, where $\mathrm{hasMarker}$ records whether the
entry is immediately followed by a $(w, \mathrm{false})$ termination marker.
Entries with index $\geq w$ that do not follow a real entry are dropped.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Triples back to an aux list]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux}
\leanok
The inverse direction of \lean{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux}: converts a list of
triples back into an aux list of $(\mathbb{N} \times \mathrm{Bool})$ entries,
emitting a $(w, \mathrm{false})$ termination marker after each triple whose
marker flag is true.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[parseAux on an entry followed by a marker]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_cons_marker}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux}
Equational lemma: if an entry $(idx, dir)$ with $idx < w$ is immediately
followed by the marker $(w, \mathrm{false})$, then $\mathrm{parseAux}$ emits the
triple $(\langle idx, h\rangle, dir, \mathrm{true})$ followed by the parse of the
rest.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[parseAux on an entry followed by a non-marker]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_cons_nonmarker}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux}
Equational lemma: if an entry $(idx, dir)$ is immediately followed by another
in-range entry $(idx', dir')$, then $\mathrm{parseAux}$ emits the triple
$(\langle idx, h\rangle, dir, \mathrm{false})$ followed by the parse of the
remaining list starting at $(idx', dir')$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[parseAux on a single entry]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_singleton}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux}
Equational lemma: a single in-range entry $(idx, dir)$ parses to the singleton
$[(\langle idx, h\rangle, dir, \mathrm{false})]$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[parseAux on the empty list]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_nil}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux}
Equational lemma: $\mathrm{parseAux}$ of the empty list is the empty list.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[parseAux inverts triplesToAux]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_triplesToAux}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.parseAux, SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_cons_marker, SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_cons_nonmarker, SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_nil, SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_singleton, SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux}
Round-trip identity: for every triple list $ts$, applying
$\mathrm{triplesToAux}$ and then $\mathrm{parseAux}$ recovers $ts$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[triplesToAux distributes over append]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux_append}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux}
$\mathrm{triplesToAux}\,(ts_1 \mathbin{+\!\!+} ts_2) = \mathrm{triplesToAux}\,ts_1 \mathbin{+\!\!+} \mathrm{triplesToAux}\,ts_2$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Combined length bound for processClauseLits]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_len_add}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
The aux output length of $\mathrm{processClauseLits}$ plus the remaining path
length never exceeds the initial path length.
\end{lemma}

\begin{definition}[Mark the last entry of a block]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.markLast}
\leanok
Builds a triple list from a $\mathrm{Fin}\,w \times \mathrm{Bool}$ block by
setting the marker flag to true on the last entry only and to false on all
others.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[triplesToAux of markLast]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux_markLast}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.markLast, SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux}
On a nonempty block, $\mathrm{triplesToAux}(\mathrm{markLast}\,block)$ equals the
block mapped into $\mathbb{N} \times \mathrm{Bool}$ followed by a single
$(w, \mathrm{false})$ termination marker.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[markLast of a nonempty list is nonempty]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.markLast_ne_nil}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.markLast}
If $block \neq []$ then $\mathrm{markLast}\,block \neq []$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[markLast preserves length]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.markLast_length}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.markLast}
$(\mathrm{markLast}\,block).\mathrm{length} = block.\mathrm{length}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Last entry of markLast is marked]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.markLast_getLast_true}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.markLast, SwitchingLemma2.markLast_ne_nil}
On a nonempty block, the last element of $\mathrm{markLast}\,block$ has marker
flag equal to true.
\end{lemma}

\begin{definition}[Cast an in-range aux list into Fin blocks]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock}
\leanok
Casts a list of $(\mathbb{N} \times \mathrm{Bool})$ entries, all of whose first
coordinates are $< w$, into a list of $(\mathrm{Fin}\,w \times \mathrm{Bool})$.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[toFinBlock preserves length]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock_length}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock}
$(\mathrm{toFinBlock}\,w\,l\,h).\mathrm{length} = l.\mathrm{length}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[toFinBlock map recovers the original list]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock_map}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock}
Mapping the $\mathrm{Fin}$ blocks back to $(\mathbb{N} \times \mathrm{Bool})$
via $p \mapsto (p.1.\mathrm{val}, p.2)$ recovers the original list $l$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[toFinBlock is nonempty for nonempty input]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock_ne_nil}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock}
If $l \neq []$ then $\mathrm{toFinBlock}\,w\,l\,h \neq []$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Aux indices bounded by term length]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_idx_lt}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_entries_from_lits, Term}
When the processed literals come from $t.\mathrm{zipIdx}$, every entry of the aux
output of $\mathrm{processClauseLits}$ has first coordinate strictly less than
$t.\mathrm{length}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoder well-formedness invariant]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_wellformed}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.width, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.Term.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_acc, SwitchingLemma2.markLast, SwitchingLemma2.markLast_getLast_true, SwitchingLemma2.markLast_length, SwitchingLemma2.markLast_ne_nil, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_idx_lt, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_len_add, SwitchingLemma2.term_length_le_width, SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock, SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock_length, SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock_map, SwitchingLemma2.toFinBlock_ne_nil, SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux, SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux_append, SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux_markLast}
The aux output of $\mathrm{razborovEncode.go}$ (started with empty accumulator)
can be written as $\mathrm{triplesToAux}\,w\,ts$ for some triple list $ts$ whose
length is bounded by the path length and whose last element, if any, has marker
flag true. This is the main structural invariant of the encoder.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Injection from encoder aux images]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.exists_aux_injection}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, DecisionTree.deepPath, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_wellformed, SwitchingLemma2.parseAux, SwitchingLemma2.parseAux_triplesToAux, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode, SwitchingLemma2.triplesToAux}
There exists a function $g$ from aux lists into
$\mathrm{Fin}\,d \to \mathrm{Fin}\,w \times \mathrm{Bool} \times \mathrm{Bool}$
that is injective on the image of the Razborov encoder over bad $s$-restrictions
with fixed $\gamma$. This isolates the combinatorial encoding of each of the $d$
path steps as a triple $(\mathrm{position}, \mathrm{direction}, \mathrm{hasMarker})$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Cardinality bound on the aux image]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.aux_image_card_bound}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.exists_aux_injection, SwitchingLemma2.fixedTerm_implies_dtDepth_zero, SwitchingLemma2.killedAll_implies_dtDepth_zero, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode, SwitchingLemma2.restrictFn, SwitchingLemma2.term_length_le_width, dtDepth}
The number of distinct aux lists produced by the Razborov encoder on bad
restrictions with a fixed first component $\gamma$ is at most $(4w)^d$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Fiber bound for the encoder]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.fiber_bound}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.IsRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.aux_image_card_bound, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode_injective}
The number of bad $s$-restrictions $\rho$ mapping to a fixed $\gamma$ under the
encoder is at most $(4w)^d$, since each of the $d$ path steps carries
$w \cdot 2 \cdot 2 = 4w$ possibilities.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Number of restrictions with $k$ free variables]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.card_filter_numFree_eq}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_path_length_eq}
The number of restrictions of $\{0,\dots,n-1\}$ with exactly $k$ free variables
equals $\binom{n}{k} \cdot 2^{\,n-k}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Updating a free variable decreases numFree by one]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.numFree_update_free}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree}
If $\rho\,v = \mathrm{none}$, then updating $\rho$ at $v$ to $\mathrm{some}\,b$
decreases $\mathrm{numFree}$ by exactly one:
$\mathrm{numFree}(\rho[v \mapsto b]) + 1 = \rho.\mathrm{numFree}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[processClauseLits preserves free-variable agreement]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_freeVars_agree}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
If $\rho_0$ and $\sigma$ have the same free variables, then after
$\mathrm{processClauseLits}$ the updated $\rho_0$ and $\sigma$ components still
agree on which variables are free.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Free-variable count of $\sigma$ after processClauseLits]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_numFree_σ}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.numFree_update_free, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
Under the invariants that $\rho_0$ and $\sigma$ share free variables and each
processed literal's variable is free in $\rho_0$, the $\sigma$-component of
$\mathrm{processClauseLits}$ has $\mathrm{numFree}$ decreased by exactly
$\min(\mathrm{lits.length}, \mathrm{path.length})$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Canonical deep path length lower bound]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_deepPath_length_ge}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DecisionTree.deepPath, DecisionTree.depth, DecisionTree.length_deepPath, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_depth_ge, SwitchingLemma2.restrictFn, dtDepth}
If $\rho$ is a bad restriction (so $\mathrm{dtDepth}(f|_\rho) > d$), then the
deepest path of the canonical decision tree $\mathrm{canonicalDTree}\,f\,\rho$
has length strictly greater than $d$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoder path length equals $d$ on bad restrictions]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode_path_length}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DecisionTree.deepPath, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_deepPath_length_ge}
When $\rho$ is bad, the prefix
$(\mathrm{canonicalDTree}\,f\,\rho).\mathrm{deepPath}.\mathrm{take}\,d$ used by the
encoder has length exactly $d$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Path length consumed by processClauseLits]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_path_length_eq}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
$\mathrm{processClauseLits}$ consumes exactly
$\min(\mathrm{lits.length}, \mathrm{path.length})$ path entries; the remaining
path length plus this minimum equals the original path length.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Updating to some decreases numFree by at most one]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.numFree_update_some_ge}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.numFree_update_free}
Updating $\rho$ at $v$ to $\mathrm{some}\,b$ decreases $\mathrm{numFree}$ by at
most one: $\mathrm{numFree}(\rho[v \mapsto b]) + 1 \geq \rho.\mathrm{numFree}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Remaining path bounded by remaining free variables]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_remaining_le_numFree}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.numFree_update_some_ge, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
If the initial path length is at most $\rho_0.\mathrm{numFree}$, then after
$\mathrm{processClauseLits}$ the remaining path length is at most the updated
$\rho_0$'s $\mathrm{numFree}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Canonical tree has depth zero when all clauses killed]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_depth_zero_of_killed}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DecisionTree.depth, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.Term.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree}
If every clause of $f$ is killed by $\rho$, the canonical decision tree for
$f|_\rho$ is the leaf $\mathrm{false}$ and hence has depth $0$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Canonical tree has depth zero when a clause is fixed]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_depth_zero_of_fixed}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DecisionTree.depth, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.Term.fixedBy, SwitchingLemma2.Term.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree}
If some clause of $f$ is fixed (satisfied) by $\rho$, the canonical decision tree
for $f|_\rho$ is the leaf $\mathrm{true}$ and hence has depth $0$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{definition}[Canonical path predicate]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.IsCanonicalPath}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DecisionTree.deepPath, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree}
$\mathrm{IsCanonicalPath}\,f\,\rho\,\mathrm{path}$ holds when $\mathrm{path}$ is an
initial segment of the canonical decision tree's deepest root-to-leaf path for
$f|_\rho$, i.e.\ $\mathrm{path} = (\mathrm{canonicalDTree}\,f\,\rho).\mathrm{deepPath}.\mathrm{take}\,(\mathrm{path.length})$.
This is the invariant carried by the Razborov encoder.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Filtered zipIdx preserves length]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_filter_length}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, Term}
Filtering $t.\mathrm{zipIdx}$ by a predicate on the literal component has the same
length as filtering the original term $t$ by the same predicate.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Filtered zipIdx first component]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_filter_getElem_fst}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, Term}
The literal component of the $k$-th element of the filtered $t.\mathrm{zipIdx}$
equals the $k$-th element of the filtered term $t$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Deep path matches free literals of the first alive clause]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_deepPath_match_freeLits}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DecisionTree, DecisionTree.deepPath, DecisionTree.depth, Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.Term.fixedBy, SwitchingLemma2.Term.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_alive_eq_termSubTree', SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_depth_zero_of_fixed, SwitchingLemma2.termSubTree_deepPath_var_match, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_filter_getElem_fst, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_filter_length, Term}
When $f.\mathrm{find?}$ returns an alive clause $t$ and $\mathrm{flis}$ is the
filtered (zipIdx) list of $t$'s free literals under $\rho$, the variable at
position $k$ of $(\mathrm{canonicalDTree}\,f\,\rho).\mathrm{deepPath}$ equals the
variable of the $k$-th free literal, since the canonical tree branches on the
free literals of $t$ in clause order.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Remaining path is a drop]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_fst_eq_drop}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
The remaining path returned by $\mathrm{processClauseLits}$ equals
$\mathrm{path}.\mathrm{drop}(\min(\mathrm{lits.length}, \mathrm{path.length}))$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Free-variable count of $\rho_0$ after processClauseLits]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_numFree_ρ_eq}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.numFree_update_free, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
Provided each literal's variable is free in $\rho_0$ and the variables are
pairwise distinct, the $\rho_0$-component of $\mathrm{processClauseLits}$ loses
exactly $\min(\mathrm{lits.length}, \mathrm{path.length})$ free variables.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[termSubTree deep path after dropping the free-literal prefix]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_termSubTree_drop}
\leanok
\uses{DecisionTree, DecisionTree.deepPath, Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.filter_free_update_eq, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.termSubTree, SwitchingLemma2.termSubTree_cons_nonfree, SwitchingLemma2.termSubTree_deepPath_head_free, Term}
Dropping the free-literal prefix from $(\mathrm{termSubTree}\,t\,\rho_0\,\mathrm{cont}).\mathrm{deepPath}$
yields the deep path of the continuation evaluated at the
$\mathrm{processClauseLits}$-updated restriction, connecting the canonical tree
structure to the encoder's restriction update.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Canonical path preserved by processClauseLits]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.canonicalPath_preserve_processClauseLits}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DecisionTree.deepPath, DecisionTree.depth, DecisionTree.length_deepPath, Literal, SwitchingLemma2.IsCanonicalPath, SwitchingLemma2.Literal.fixedBy, SwitchingLemma2.Literal.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.Term.fixedBy, SwitchingLemma2.Term.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_alive_eq_termSubTree', SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_depth_zero_of_fixed, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_depth_zero_of_killed, SwitchingLemma2.cont_eq_canonicalDTree, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_fst_eq_drop, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_numFree_ρ_eq, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_termSubTree_drop, SwitchingLemma2.termSubTree, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_filter_getElem_fst, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_filter_length, Term}
When the literals are the free literals of the first alive clause and the input
path is canonical, $\mathrm{IsCanonicalPath}$ is preserved by
$\mathrm{processClauseLits}$: the remaining path is again a canonical path for the
updated restriction, and its length is at most the depth of the updated canonical
tree.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Free literals have pairwise distinct variables]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_freeLits_pairwise_var}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, Term}
If $t$ has no duplicate literals and any two literals of $t$ with the same
variable are equal, then the free-variable filter of $t.\mathrm{zipIdx}$ has
pairwise distinct variables.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoder free-variable consumption invariant]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode_go_numFree_invariant}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DecisionTree.deepPath, DecisionTree.depth, DecisionTree.length_deepPath, Literal, SwitchingLemma2.IsCanonicalPath, SwitchingLemma2.Literal.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.Term.fixedBy, SwitchingLemma2.Term.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_deepPath_match_freeLits, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_depth_zero_of_fixed, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_depth_zero_of_killed, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalPath_preserve_processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.choose_mul_pow_bound, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_acc, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_freeLits_pairwise_var, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_freeVars_agree, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_len_add, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_numFree_σ, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_path_le, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_path_length_eq}
When the canonical tree depth is at least the path length,
$\mathrm{razborovEncode.go}$ fully consumes the path (never terminating early),
and the resulting $\sigma$ has $\mathrm{numFree}$ decreased by exactly the initial
path length: $(\mathrm{go}\,\dots).1.\mathrm{numFree} + \mathrm{path.length} = \sigma.\mathrm{numFree}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoder output is an $(s-d)$-restriction]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode_fst_numFree_eq}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DecisionTree.deepPath, DecisionTree.depth, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.IsCanonicalPath, SwitchingLemma2.IsRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.numFree, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree_depth_ge, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode_go_numFree_invariant, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode_path_length, SwitchingLemma2.restrictFn, dtDepth}
For a bad $s$-restriction $\rho$ with $d \leq s$, the first component
(the $\gamma$-output) of $\mathrm{razborovEncode}\,f\,w\,d\,\rho$ is an
$(s-d)$-restriction, since the encoder fixes exactly $d$ previously free
variables.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[No bad restrictions when $s \leq d$]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.bad_filter_empty_of_d_ge_s}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.IsRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.dtDepth_restrictFn_le_numFree}
If $s \leq d$, the set of restrictions that are simultaneously $s$-restrictions
and bad restrictions for depth $d$ is empty.
\end{lemma}

\begin{definition}[Decision tree to DNF]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.toDNF}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DecisionTree}
Converts a decision tree into a DNF whose terms are the conjunctions of literals
along each root-to-leaf path ending in a $\mathrm{true}$ leaf.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Decision tree to CNF]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.toCNF}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, DecisionTree}
Converts a decision tree into a CNF whose clauses are the disjunctions of negated
literals along each root-to-leaf path ending in a $\mathrm{false}$ leaf.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Decision-tree-depth witness tree]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.dtDepth_witness}
\leanok
\uses{DecisionTree, DecisionTree.depth, DecisionTree.eval, buildFullDTree, buildFullDTree_depth, buildFullDTree_eval, dtDepth}
For any Boolean function $f$ there exists a decision tree $T$ computing $f$ with
depth at most $\mathrm{dtDepth}\,f$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Small decision-tree depth yields small DNF and CNF]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.dtDepth_le_implies_small_dnf_cnf}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.eval, CNF.evalClause, CNF.width, DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, DecisionTree, DecisionTree.depth, DecisionTree.eval, Literal.eval, SwitchingLemma2.dtDepth_witness, SwitchingLemma2.toCNF, SwitchingLemma2.toDNF, Term.eval, dtDepth}
If $\mathrm{dtDepth}\,f \leq d$, then $f$ is computed by a DNF of width at most
$d$ and by a CNF of width at most $d$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{definition}[Flip a literal's polarity]
\lean{Literal.flipNeg}
\leanok
\uses{Literal}
Negates a literal by flipping its polarity bit while keeping its variable.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[flipNeg negates the literal value]
\lean{Literal.flipNeg_eval}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, Literal.eval, Literal.flipNeg}
$(l.\mathrm{flipNeg}).\mathrm{eval}\,x = \lnot\,(l.\mathrm{eval}\,x)$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[flipNeg preserves the variable]
\lean{Literal.flipNeg_var}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, Literal.flipNeg}
$(l.\mathrm{flipNeg}).\mathrm{var} = l.\mathrm{var}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[flipNeg is injective]
\lean{Literal.flipNeg_injective}
\leanok
\uses{Literal.flipNeg}
The map $\mathrm{Literal.flipNeg}$ is injective.
\end{lemma}

\begin{definition}[CNF to De Morgan dual DNF]
\lean{cnfToDualDNF}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, DNF, Literal.flipNeg}
Converts a CNF $\psi$ to its De Morgan dual DNF by negating every literal, so each
clause (a disjunction) becomes a term (a conjunction) of negated literals:
$\lnot\bigl(\bigwedge_i \bigvee_j l_{ij}\bigr) = \bigvee_i \bigl(\bigwedge_j \lnot l_{ij}\bigr)$.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[cnfToDualDNF preserves width]
\lean{cnfToDualDNF_width}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.width, DNF.width, Term.width, cnfToDualDNF}
$(\mathrm{cnfToDualDNF}\,\psi).\mathrm{width} = \psi.\mathrm{width}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[cnfToDualDNF negates the value]
\lean{cnfToDualDNF_eval}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.eval, CNF.evalClause, DNF.eval, Literal.flipNeg_eval, Term.eval, cnfToDualDNF}
$(\mathrm{cnfToDualDNF}\,\psi).\mathrm{eval}\,x = \lnot\,(\psi.\mathrm{eval}\,x)$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{definition}[Negate decision tree leaves]
\lean{DecisionTree.negateLeaves}
\leanok
\uses{DecisionTree}
Negates every leaf of a decision tree, leaving its branching structure unchanged.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[negateLeaves negates the value]
\lean{DecisionTree.negateLeaves_eval}
\leanok
\uses{DecisionTree, DecisionTree.eval, DecisionTree.negateLeaves}
$T.\mathrm{negateLeaves}.\mathrm{eval}\,x = \lnot\,(T.\mathrm{eval}\,x)$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[negateLeaves preserves depth]
\lean{DecisionTree.negateLeaves_depth}
\leanok
\uses{DecisionTree, DecisionTree.depth, DecisionTree.negateLeaves}
$T.\mathrm{negateLeaves}.\mathrm{depth} = T.\mathrm{depth}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Decision-tree depth is invariant under negation]
\lean{dtDepth_neg}
\leanok
\uses{DecisionTree, DecisionTree.depth, DecisionTree.eval, DecisionTree.negateLeaves, buildFullDTree, buildFullDTree_depth, buildFullDTree_eval, dtDepth}
$\mathrm{dtDepth}(\lnot f) = \mathrm{dtDepth}\,f$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Restriction commutes with negation]
\lean{SwitchingLemmaCNF.restrictFn_neg}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.restrictFn}
$\mathrm{restrictFn}(\lnot f)\,\rho = \lnot\,(\mathrm{restrictFn}\,f\,\rho)$,
pointwise.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Bad restriction is invariant under negation]
\lean{SwitchingLemmaCNF.IsBadRestriction_neg}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemmaCNF.restrictFn_neg, dtDepth_neg}
$\mathrm{IsBadRestriction}(\lnot f)\,d\,\rho \iff \mathrm{IsBadRestriction}\,f\,d\,\rho$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Dual DNF preserves no-duplicate clauses]
\lean{SwitchingLemmaCNF.cnfToDualDNF_nodup}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, Literal.flipNeg_injective, cnfToDualDNF}
If every clause of $\psi$ has no duplicate literals, then every term of
$\mathrm{cnfToDualDNF}\,\psi$ has no duplicate literals.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Dual DNF preserves variable-distinctness]
\lean{SwitchingLemmaCNF.cnfToDualDNF_inj}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, Literal.flipNeg_var, cnfToDualDNF}
If within each clause of $\psi$ any two literals with the same variable are equal,
then the same holds within every term of $\mathrm{cnfToDualDNF}\,\psi$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[Håstad's Switching Lemma for CNFs]
\lean{SwitchingLemmaCNF.switching_lemma_cnf}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.eval, CNF.width, DNF.eval, DNF.width, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.IsRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.numSRestrictions, SwitchingLemma2.switching_lemma, SwitchingLemmaCNF.cnfToDualDNF_inj, SwitchingLemmaCNF.cnfToDualDNF_nodup, SwitchingLemmaCNF.restrictFn_neg, cnfToDualDNF, cnfToDualDNF_eval, cnfToDualDNF_width, dtDepth_neg}
For a CNF $\psi$ of width at most $w$ on $n$ variables with $5s \leq n$ and clauses
free of duplicate or conflicting variables, the number of $s$-restrictions $\rho$
under which $\psi|_\rho$ has decision-tree depth $> d$ satisfies
\[
  \abs{\{\rho : \mathrm{IsRestriction}\,s\,\rho \wedge \mathrm{IsBadRestriction}\,\psi.\mathrm{eval}\,d\,\rho\}} \cdot n^d
  \;\leq\; \mathrm{numSRestrictions}\,n\,s \cdot (10\,s\,w)^d .
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Switching Lemma Corollary for CNFs]
\lean{SwitchingLemmaCNF.switching_corollary_cnf}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.eval, CNF.width, DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, SwitchingLemma2.IsRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.dtDepth_le_implies_small_dnf_cnf, SwitchingLemma2.numSRestrictions, SwitchingLemma2.restrictFn, SwitchingLemmaCNF.switching_lemma_cnf, dtDepth}
For a CNF $\psi$ of width at most $w$ with $5s \leq n$ and well-formed clauses, the
number of $s$-restrictions $\rho$ for which $\psi|_\rho$ cannot be represented by a
DNF of width $\leq w$ satisfies
\[
  \abs{\{\rho : \mathrm{IsRestriction}\,s\,\rho \wedge \lnot\,\exists \varphi,\ \varphi.\mathrm{width} \leq w \wedge \varphi \equiv \psi|_\rho\}} \cdot n^w
  \;\leq\; \mathrm{numSRestrictions}\,n\,s \cdot (10\,s\,w)^w .
\]
\end{theorem}
